items.text: Lloyd's Coffee House in London - Birthplace of modern marine insurance.
Oriental Life Insurance Company - First Life Company in India (started by Europeans).
Bombay Mutual Life Assurance Society - First Indian-owned life insurance company.
245 companies merged to form LIC (Nationalization of Life Insurance).
General Insurance Business Act - Nationalization of Non-Life Insurance.
IRDAI Established - The Supreme Regulator of Insurance in India.

cards.title: 1. Risk Avoidance (Negative)
cards.content: Avoiding the activity altogether (e.g., Not traveling to avoid accidents). It is a negative way to handle risk.

cards.title: 2. Risk Retention (Self-Insurance)
cards.content: Deciding to bear the loss yourself. Best for small, predictable losses.

cards.title: 3. Risk Reduction (Practical)
cards.content: Lowering the chance of loss (e.g., Using fire extinguishers or wearing helmets).

cards.title: 4. Risk Transfer (The BEST)
cards.content: Transferring the financial burden to an Insurance Company.

cards.title: Law of Large Numbers
cards.content: Predicting the future becomes easier when we look at a large group of people.

cards.title: Pooling of Risks
cards.content: The 'Many' contribute premiums to pay for the losses of the 'Few'.

cards.title: Human Life Value (HLV)
cards.content: Introduced by Prof. S.S. Huebner. It measures the economic value of a human life based on future earnings.

sections.title: 🚀 Exam Pro-Tips (Quick Memory)
items: IRDAI is the ONLY regulator in India. (Established in 2000).
Insurance is a 'Tool for Risk Transfer', not a tool to remove the risk itself.
Primary Burden of Risk: The actual financial loss.
Secondary Burden of Risk: The mental strain, anxiety, and keeping reserve funds.
Don't confuse LIC (1956) with GIC (1972) dates in MCQs!

cards.title: 1. Reliability
cards.content: The ability to perform the promised service dependably and accurately. (e.g., Doing what you promised).

cards.title: 2. Responsiveness
cards.content: Willingness to help customers and provide prompt service. (e.g., Replying to calls/emails quickly).

cards.title: 3. Assurance
cards.content: Employees' knowledge, courtesy, and ability to convey trust and confidence.

cards.title: 4. Empathy
cards.content: Caring, individualized attention given to customers. (Putting yourself in their shoes).

cards.title: 5. Tangibles
cards.content: Physical facilities, equipment, and appearance of personnel. (Since insurance is intangible, documents must look professional).

cards.title: IGMS (Integrated Grievance Management System)
cards.content: An online portal launched by IRDAI. It acts as a central repository for industry-wide consumer grievances.

cards.title: Consumer Courts (CPA)
cards.content: Three-tier system: District Commission, State Commission, and National Commission. Used when customers are dissatisfied with the service.

cards.title: Insurance Ombudsman
cards.content: Created by the Government. Resolves complaints out of the court system efficiently and impartially.

items.text: Who can approach? Any person who has a grievance against an insurer.
Complaint must be made within 1 YEAR of the insurance company rejecting the complaint.
Maximum compensation awarded by Ombudsman cannot exceed ₹30 Lakhs.
There is NO FEE charged for filing a complaint with the Ombudsman.
The Ombudsman's decision is binding on the insurer, but NOT binding on the customer.

sections.title: 🚀 Exam Pro-Tips (Quick Memory)
items: Active Listening is the most important communication skill for an agent.
Insurance is an 'Intangible' product. You are selling a promise.
CLV (Customer Lifetime Value) is the historical and future revenue expected from a customer.
If a customer's complaint is not resolved within 15 days by the company, they can go to the Ombudsman.
Ombudsman has 1 month to give a recommendation, and 3 months to pass an award.

cards.title: High Weightage Areas
cards.content:
• Insurance Ombudsman (Limits, Fees, and Timelines)
• Consumer Protection Act 3-Tier System (District, State, National)
• IGMS (Integrated Grievance Management System)

cards.title: 1. IGMS (Integrated Grievance Management System)
cards.content: Launched by IRDAI to track and monitor grievance redressal in the insurance industry. It acts as a central repository for all insurance grievances in India. Customers can register complaints directly with the insurer or escalate to IRDAI.

cards.title: 2. Consumer Protection Act (3-Tier System)
cards.content:
District Commission: Up to Rs. 1 Crore.
State Commission: Rs. 1 Crore to Rs. 10 Crores.
National Commission: Exceeds Rs. 10 Crores.
Key Point: A 'Consumer' is someone who buys for personal use, NOT commercial purposes.

cards.title: 3. The Insurance Ombudsman
cards.content:
Quick, impartial settlement for life & general insurance.
Financial Limit: Max award is Rs. 30 Lakhs.
Fees: NO fee to file a complaint.
Time Limit: Within 1 year of insurer rejection.
Binding Nature: Binding on company, but policyholder can still approach consumer court.

sections.title: 🚀 Exam Pro-Tips (Repeated Themes)
items: Maximum Ombudsman Award: Rs. 30 Lakhs.
Fee for Ombudsman: Zero (No Fee).
Time limit to file with Ombudsman: 1 Year after rejection.
District Commission handles disputes up to Rs. 1 Crore.

sections.title: Chapter 4: Regulatory Aspects of Insurance Agents

cards.title: High Weightage Areas
cards.content:
• Prerequisites and Validity of License
• Section 41 (Prohibition of Rebates)
• IRDAI Code of Conduct
• Conditions for Disqualification

cards.title: 1. Agency License Rules
cards.content:
Age: Min 18 years.
Qualification: Min 10th standard.
Validity: 3 Years.
Composite Agent: Can hold 1 Life, 1 General, 1 Health, and 1 Mono-line license simultaneously, but NOT two of the same category.

cards.title: 2. Code of Conduct (Do's and Don'ts)
cards.content:
• Must show ID card and disclose commission if asked.
• Churning/Twisting: Forcing customer to end policy to buy new one (Strictly Prohibited).
• Material Facts: Agents must ensure 100% accurate disclosure.

cards.title: 3. Section 41: Prohibition of Rebates
cards.content:
Most Important! Prohibits offering or taking discounts (rebates) to induce a purchase.
Penalty: Fine up to Rs. 10 Lakhs.

cards.title: 4. Disqualification
cards.content: Disqualified if: Minor, Unsound mind, guilty of fraud, or breach of trust. License can be suspended or cancelled for code violations.

sections.title: 🚀 Exam Pro-Tips (Repeated Themes)
items: License Validity: 3 Years.
Anti-Rebate Section: Section 41 of Insurance Act, 1938.
Max Rebate Penalty: Rs. 10 Lakhs.
Wait period for policy replacement (Churning): 3 Years.

sections.title: Chapter 5: Legal Principles of an Insurance Contract

cards.title: Core Legal Principles
cards.content:
• Utmost Good Faith (Uberrima Fides)
• Insurable Interest (Timing Rules)
• Principle of Indemnity (Exceptions)
• Proximate Cause (Causa Proxima)

cards.title: 1. Utmost Good Faith (Uberrima Fides)
cards.content: Proposer must truthfully disclose all 'Material Facts'. Concealment (e.g., hiding diabetes) makes the policy void.

cards.title: 2. Insurable Interest (TIMING RULES)
cards.content:
• Life Insurance: Must exist only at the START of the policy.
• Marine Insurance: Must exist at the time of LOSS.
• Fire Insurance: Must exist at both START and LOSS.

cards.title: 3. Principle of Indemnity
cards.content: To compensate the loss, not to make profit. Does NOT apply to Life Insurance and Personal Accident Insurance.

cards.title: 4. Proximate Cause & Others
cards.content:
Proximate Cause: The direct, dominant cause of loss.
Subrogation: Insurer steps into insured's shoes to recover from 3rd party.
Contribution: Multiple insurers share loss proportionately.
(Subrogation/Contribution do NOT apply to Life Insurance).

sections.title: 🚀 Exam Pro-Tips (Repeated Themes)
items: Insurable Interest in Life: Required at the time of taking the policy.
Indemnity in Life Insurance: Does NOT apply.
Uberrima Fides: Utmost Good Faith.
Causa Proxima: The most effective cause of loss.

cards.title: Core Concepts of Life Insurance
cards.content:
• Human Life Value (HLV) - Prof. Hubner
• Mutuality and Risk Pooling
• Level Premium System vs. Natural Premium

cards.title: 1. What is HLV?
cards.content: Developed by Prof. Hubner. It measures a person's economic value based on future net earnings. It answers: 'How much insurance do I need?'

cards.title: 2. Risk Pooling & Mutuality
cards.content: Foundation of insurance. The 'Many' contribute to a common fund to share the losses of the unfortunate 'Few'.

cards.title: 3. Natural vs. Level Premium
cards.content:
Natural Premium: Increases every year as risk (age) increases.
Level Premium: Remains constant throughout the term. Policyholders pay MORE in early years to cover high risk in later years.

cards.title: 4. Cash Value & Reserve
cards.content: In Level Premium, the excess money collected in early years creates a reserve. This build-up is known as the 'Cash Value' or 'Surrender Value'.

sections.title: 🚀 Exam Pro-Tips (Repeated Themes)
items: HLV Concept Father: Prof. Hubner.
System with constant premium: Level Premium System.
Cash Value Reason: Excess premium collected in early years of Level Premium policies.
Life Insurance Contract: Long-term protection + savings.

cards.title: Core of Financial Planning
cards.content:
• Economic Life Cycle (Phases of Life)
• Contingency Funds & Emergency Needs
• Impact of Inflation on Purchasing Power
• Role of Insurance vs. Investment

cards.title: 1. Phases of Life
cards.content:
Student Phase: Preparation & Education.
Working Phase: Wealth Accumulation & Protection.
Retirement Phase: Wealth Distribution & Conservation.

cards.title: 2. When to Start?
cards.content: The best time to start financial planning is 'as soon as one starts earning'. Don't wait for marriage or retirement.

cards.title: 3. Contingency vs. Retirement
cards.content:
Contingency: Managed by Emergency Funds & Insurance.
Retirement: Managed by Pensions & Annuities (covers risk of 'living too long').

cards.title: 4. Impact of Inflation
cards.content: Inflation reduces 'Purchasing Power'. Real rate of return = Interest Rate - Inflation Rate. Planning must account for this.

sections.title: 🚀 Exam Pro-Tips (Repeated Themes)
items: Starting Time: Immediately after the first salary.
Outliving savings risk cover: Annuities / Pensions.
Inflation effect: Reduces purchasing power of money.
Contingency fund goal: To handle emergencies without breaking long-term investments.

cards.title: Q1. Best time to start financial planning?
cards.content: Answer: As soon as one gets their first salary.

cards.title: Q2. Primarily a protection product?
cards.content: Answer: Term Life Insurance.

cards.title: Product Categories
cards.content:
• Term Insurance (Pure Risk Cover)
• Endowment Plans (Protection + Savings)
• Whole Life Policies (Estate Creation)
• Riders (Add-on Benefits)

cards.title: 1. Term Insurance
cards.content: Simplest & Cheapest. Highest risk cover for lowest premium. NO maturity/savings benefit. (If you survive, nothing is paid).

cards.title: 2. Endowment Insurance
cards.content: Term Insurance + Pure Endowment. Benefit paid on EITHER death OR survival (maturity). Good for targeted savings.

cards.title: 3. Whole Life Insurance
cards.content: Covers entire life (up to 100 years). Best for 'Estate Creation' and leaving a legacy for heirs.

cards.title: Optional Benefits
cards.content: Additional covers like Accidental Death, Critical Illness, Waiver of Premium (WOP). Nominal extra cost.

cards.title: Important Rule
cards.content: Premium for all health/critical illness riders cannot exceed 30% of the base policy premium.

sections.title: 🚀 Exam Pro-Tips (Repeated Themes)
items: Highest coverage @ Lowest premium: Term Insurance.
Maturity benefit absent in: Term Insurance.
Legacy/Estate creation policy: Whole Life.
Endowment combo: Term Insurance + Pure Endowment.

cards.title: Q1. Agent recommendation for lowest premium/high cover?
cards.content: Answer: Term Insurance.

cards.title: Q2. What is a 'Rider'?
cards.content: Answer: An add-on benefit attached to the base policy for extra premium.

cards.title: Market-Linked Products
cards.content:
• Traditional vs Non-Traditional Products
• Unit Linked Insurance Plans (ULIPs)
• Net Asset Value (NAV) Calculation
• Universal Life Insurance Features

cards.title: 1. Why Non-Traditional?
cards.content: Traditional products are 'bundled' (opaque). Non-traditional products bring Transparency through 'Unbundling'—separating risk cover, expenses, and investment.

cards.title: 2. ULIP Core Concepts
cards.content:
Premiums buy 'Units' in a fund (Equity, Debt, etc.). 
NAV: The price per unit.
Risk: Investment risk is 100% borne by the POLICYHOLDER.
Lock-in: Mandatory 5 years.

cards.title: 3. Flexibility in ULIPs
cards.content:
Fund Switching: Move money between Equity & Debt.
Premium Holiday: Temporary stop in payments if fund value is high enough.

cards.title: 4. Key Features
cards.content: Highly flexible. Allows policyholders to change the premium amount and Sum Assured based on their financial life changes.

sections.title: 🚀 Exam Pro-Tips (Repeated Themes)
items: ULIP Investment Risk: Borne by the Policyholder.
Unbundling: Separating protection, savings, and expenses.
ULIP Value: Depends on the NAV of the units.
Switching option: Only available in ULIPs.

cards.title: Q1. Who bears the investment risk in a ULIP?
cards.content: Answer: The Policyholder.

cards.title: Q2. Advantage of non-traditional products?
cards.content: Answer: Complete unbundling and transparency.

cards.title: Legal & Business Applications
cards.content:
• Married Women's Property (MWP) Act, 1874
• Keyman Insurance (Business Protection)
• Mortgage Redemption Insurance (MRI)

cards.title: 1. Section 6 - The Trust
cards.content: Creates a 'Trust' for wife/children. The policyholder loses control—cannot surrender or take a loan without trustee consent.

cards.title: 2. Protection from Creditors
cards.content: CRITICAL: The money is 100% protected from the husband's creditors and court attachments. Even parents/relatives cannot claim it.

cards.title: 3. Keyman Insurance
cards.content: Taken by Employer on the life of an important employee. Premium is a business expense. Claim is paid to the EMPLOYER, not the family.

cards.title: 4. Mortgage Redemption (MRI)
cards.content: A Decreasing Term Insurance. Cover decreases as the loan amount decreases. Pays off the loan directly to the bank if the borrower dies.

sections.title: 🚀 Exam Pro-Tips (Repeated Themes)
items: MWP Act Protection: Protects from creditors/court attachments.
Keyman Benefit Receiver: The Employer/Company.
MRI Policy Type: Decreasing Term Insurance.
MWP Trustee Consent: Needed for any changes to the policy.

cards.title: Q1. Primary goal of MWP Act?
cards.content: Answer: Protection from Policyholder's Creditors.

cards.title: Q2. Who pays & receives in Keyman?
cards.content: Answer: Employer pays, Employer receives.

cards.title: Core Concepts
cards.content:
• Components of Premium
• Net Premium vs. Gross Premium (Formulas)
• Valuation and Surplus (Actuary's Role)
• Types of Bonuses

cards.title: 1. Components
cards.content:
Mortality: Cost of death risk.
Interest: Discount from investment earnings.
Expenses: Cost of running business.

cards.title: 2. The Formulas (Must Remember!)
cards.content:
Net Premium = Mortality - Interest
Gross Premium = Net Premium + Expenses

cards.title: 3. The Actuary's Role
cards.content: An 'Actuary' is the specialist who calculates premiums and conducts valuation (comparing Assets vs Liabilities).

cards.title: 4. Surplus & Bonus
cards.content: Surplus = Assets - Liabilities. This surplus is given to 'Participating' policyholders as a Bonus.

cards.title: Allocation Methods
cards.content:
• Simple Reversionary: % of Sum Assured only.
• Compound Reversionary: % of (Sum Assured + Previous Bonuses).
• Terminal Bonus: Paid only when policy ends.

sections.title: 🚀 Exam Pro-Tips (Repeated Themes)
items: Gross Premium formula: Net Premium + Expenses.
Premium professional: Actuary.
Surplus definition: Excess of Assets over Liabilities.
Interest effect: Always reduces the premium amount.

cards.title: Q1. Correct formula for Net Premium?
cards.content: Answer: Mortality - Interest.

cards.title: Q2. Who calculates premiums?
cards.content: Answer: Actuary.

cards.title: Initial Documents
cards.content:
• Standard vs. Non-Standard Age Proofs
• The Proposal Form (Contract Base)
• FPR vs. RPR (Proof of Risk)
• KYC and Moral Hazard Report (MHR)

cards.title: 1. Standard Age Proofs
cards.content: Issued by Public Authority. Examples: Passport, PAN Card, Birth Certificate, School Leaving Certificate.

cards.title: 2. Non-Standard Age Proofs
cards.content: Less Reliable. Examples: Horoscope, Ration Card, Village Panchayat Certificate, Self-declaration. (Company may charge extra premium).

cards.title: 3. The Proposal Form
cards.content: The BASE of the insurance contract. Every detail must be truthful (Utmost Good Faith).

cards.title: 4. FPR (First Premium Receipt)
cards.content: Conclusive legal proof that the contract has begun and risk is covered.

cards.title: Moral Hazard Report (MHR)
cards.content: Confidential report by the Agent about the proposer's lifestyle, habits, and financial status.

sections.title: 🚀 Exam Pro-Tips (Repeated Themes)
items: Non-Standard Proofs: Horoscope / Ration Card.
Contract Base: Proposal Form.
Proof of Risk Commencement: First Premium Receipt (FPR).
MHR Prepared By: Insurance Agent / Official.

cards.title: Q1. Which is a Standard Age Proof?
cards.content: Answer: Passport.

cards.title: Q2. Document signifying risk acceptance?
cards.content: Answer: First Premium Receipt (FPR).

cards.title: Policy Rules & Timelines
cards.content:
• Free Look Period (Cooling-off)
• Grace Period (Timelines by Mode)
• Lapse and Revival
• Standard Parts of a Policy Document

cards.title: 1. Parts of Document
cards.content:
Preamble: Intro of parties.
Operative Clause: The heart of the promise.
Policy Schedule: PERSONALIZED details (Name, Age, Sum Assured, Nominee).

cards.title: 2. Free Look Period
cards.content: Trial period of 15 days from receipt. Can return policy for full refund (minus stamp duty/medical/mortality charges).

cards.title: 3. Grace Period Rules
cards.content:
Monthly Mode: 15 Days.
Quarterly/Half-Yearly/Yearly: 30 Days.
Note: Claim is PAID if death occurs during grace period.

cards.title: 4. Reinstatement
cards.content: Policy lapses after grace period. Revival requires paying arrears + interest + declaration of good health.

sections.title: 🚀 Exam Pro-Tips (Repeated Themes)
items: Grace Period (Monthly): 15 Days.
Free Look Period: 15 Days.
Personalized part: Policy Schedule.
Death during grace period: Claim paid minus due premium.

cards.title: Q1. Free Look provision duration?
cards.content: Answer: 15 days.

cards.title: Q2. Grace period for yearly premium?
cards.content: Answer: 30 days.

cards.title: Legal Rights & Values
cards.content:
• Nomination (Section 39) vs. Assignment (Section 38)
• Absolute vs. Conditional Assignment
• Surrender Value and Paid-Up Value
• Policy Loans and Foreclosure

cards.title: Key Points
cards.content: Nominee is only a receiver/trustee, NOT the owner. Can be changed anytime. If nominee is minor, an 'Appointee' must be named.

cards.title: 1. Absolute Assignment
cards.content: Transfer of complete ownership. CRITICAL: This automatically CANCELS existing nomination.

cards.title: 2. Conditional Assignment
cards.content: Subject to conditions (e.g., Bank Loan). Policy returns to owner once condition is met (loan repaid).

cards.title: 3. Surrender vs Paid-Up
cards.content:
Surrender: Terminating for cash. 
Paid-Up: Continuing with reduced Sum Assured when premiums stop after 2-3 years.

cards.title: 4. Loans & Foreclosure
cards.content: Can take loan up to 90% of Surrender Value. Foreclosure occurs if (Loan + Interest) > Surrender Value.

sections.title: 🚀 Exam Pro-Tips (Repeated Themes)
items: Nomination Section: 39.
Assignment Section: 38.
Absolute Assignment impact: Cancels Nomination.
Appointee needed: When nominee is a minor.

cards.title: Q1. Bank loan assignment type?
cards.content: Answer: Conditional Assignment.

cards.title: Q2. Step for minor nominee?
cards.content: Answer: Appoint an Appointee.

cards.title: Risk Assessment Process
cards.content:
• Agent as the Primary Underwriter
• Physical Hazards vs. Moral Hazards
• Numerical Rating System
• Underwriting Decisions (Loading, Lien, etc.)

cards.title: 1. Field Underwriter (Agent)
cards.content: The agent is the FIRST or PRIMARY underwriter. Only the agent can observe the customer's lifestyle and habits directly.

cards.title: 2. Desk Underwriter
cards.content: The office official who makes the final decision based on all reports (Proposal, MHR, Medical).

cards.title: 3. Physical vs Moral Hazards
cards.content:
Physical: Body/Environment (BP, Diabetes, Mining Job).
Moral: Character/Habits (Hiding illness, excessive drinking, insurance beyond income).

cards.title: 4. Numerical Rating System
cards.content: Developed by Dr. Arthur Hunter. Points are given for risk factors. Standard risk score = 100.

cards.title: Decision Types
cards.content:
• Standard Acceptance: Regular premium.
• Loading: Extra premium for higher risk.
• Lien: Claim is reduced if death occurs early.
• Postpone/Decline: Risk is too high.

sections.title: 🚀 Exam Pro-Tips (Repeated Themes)
items: First Underwriter: The Insurance Agent.
Hiding illness: Moral Hazard.
Numerical Rating Father: Dr. Arthur Hunter.
Extra premium for health: Loading.

cards.title: Q1. Who is the 'First Underwriter'?
cards.content: Answer: The Insurance Agent.

cards.title: Q2. Condition for reduced claim early on?
cards.content: Answer: Lien.

cards.title: Claim Rules & Timelines
cards.content:
• Maturity vs. Death Claims
• Early vs. Non-Early Death Claims (3-Year Rule)
• Section 45 (Indisputability Clause)
• IRDAI Settlement Timelines & Penalties

cards.title: 1. Maturity & Death Claims
cards.content:
Maturity: Survival benefit. Requires 'Discharge Voucher'.
Death Claim: Paid to nominee on death during term.
Early Claim: Death within 3 YEARS of policy start/revival. (Strict Investigation).

cards.title: 2. Section 45 (Indisputability)
cards.content: A policy CANNOT be questioned on any ground (even fraud) after 3 YEARS. This is the ultimate protection for policyholders.

cards.title: Claim Settlement Time
cards.content:
30 DAYS from receiving all documents. 
90 DAYS for investigation (if needed for early claims).

cards.title: Delay Penalty
cards.content: Bank Rate + 2% Interest must be paid by the insurer if settlement is delayed.

sections.title: 🚀 Exam Pro-Tips (Repeated Themes)
items: Investigation Limit: 90 Days.
Standard Settlement: 30 Days.
Indisputability Period (Section 45): 3 Years.
Delay Penalty Interest: Bank Rate + 2%.

cards.title: Q1. Early death claim period?
cards.content: Answer: 3 Years.

cards.title: Q2. Interest on delayed claim?
cards.content: Answer: Bank Rate + 2%.

cards.title: Healthcare Fundamentals
cards.content:
• Levels of Healthcare (Primary, Secondary, Tertiary)
• Determinants of Health (Genetics vs. Lifestyle)
• Medical Inflation & Out-of-Pocket Expenses (OOP)

cards.title: 1. Primary Healthcare
cards.content: First point of contact. Common ailments. Examples: PHCs, family doctors, dispensaries.

cards.title: 2. Secondary Healthcare
cards.content: Care by specialists (Cardiologists, Surgeons) in general hospitals. Usually referred by primary care.

cards.title: 3. Tertiary Healthcare
cards.content: Highly specialized. Complex surgeries (Cancer, Organ Transplants). Examples: Super-specialty hospitals like AIIMS.

cards.title: Determinants of Health
cards.content: Genetics, Environment, and Lifestyle. Lifestyle Diseases (Diabetes, Heart Disease) are the biggest modern drivers for insurance.

cards.title: Medical Inflation
cards.content: Medical costs rise much faster than general inflation. Health insurance protects against financial ruin from Out-of-Pocket (OOP) expenses.

sections.title: 🚀 Exam Pro-Tips (Repeated Themes)
items: First point of contact: Primary Healthcare.
Organ transplant level: Tertiary Healthcare.
Medical Inflation: Rising faster than general inflation.
Lifestyle Disease example: Type-2 Diabetes.

cards.title: Q1. Complex brain surgery level?
cards.content: Answer: Tertiary Healthcare.

cards.title: Q2. Which is a Lifestyle Disease?
cards.content: Answer: Type-2 Diabetes.

cards.title: Rules & Timelines
cards.content:
• Waiting Periods (Initial, Specific, PED)
• Portability (45-Day Rule)
• Pre-Insurance Medical Checks
• Grace Period & Free Look Differences

cards.title: 1. Initial (30 Days)
cards.content:
No illness claims for first 30 days. 
EXCEPTION: Accidents are covered from DAY 1.

cards.title: 2. Specific & PED
cards.content:
Specific Diseases: 1-2 Years (Cataract, Hernia).
Pre-Existing Disease (PED): Usually 48 Months waiting period.

cards.title: The 45-Day Rule
cards.content: Must apply for portability at least 45 DAYS before renewal date. Benefits of completed waiting periods are transferred.

cards.title: Important Difference
cards.content:
Free Look: 15 Days.
Grace Period: 30 Days. 
NOTE: Unlike Life Insurance, claims are NOT payable during the grace period in Health Insurance.

sections.title: 🚀 Exam Pro-Tips (Repeated Themes)
items: Initial Illness Waiting Period: 30 Days.
Accidents: Covered from Day 1.
Portability application: 45 Days before renewal.
Claim during Grace Period: NOT payable in Health.

cards.title: Q1. Portability request timeline?
cards.content: Answer: 45 days before renewal.

cards.title: Q2. Cataract waiting period?
cards.content: Answer: 1 to 2 Years.

cards.title: Health Product Core
cards.content:
• Indemnity vs. Fixed Benefit Policies
• Family Floater (Premium Rules)
• Arogya Sanjeevani (Standard Product)
• Day Care vs. Domiciliary Hospitalization

cards.title: 1. Indemnity vs Fixed Benefit
cards.content:
Indemnity: Reimburses ACTUAL bills (Standard Mediclaim).
Fixed Benefit: Pays fixed LUMPSUM regardless of bills (Critical Illness).

cards.title: 2. Family Floater Policy
cards.content: One Sum Insured for whole family. CRITICAL: Premium is based on the age of the OLDEST member.

cards.title: 3. Arogya Sanjeevani
cards.content: Mandatory standard product. Co-payment: 5% on all claims is mandatory (Insured pays 5%).

cards.title: 4. Day Care & Domiciliary
cards.content:
Day Care: Less than 24 hrs (Cataract, Dialysis).
Domiciliary: Treatment at HOME (Must be for at least 3 days).

sections.title: 🚀 Exam Pro-Tips (Repeated Themes)
items: Family Floater Premium: Based on OLDEST member.
Critical Illness type: Fixed Benefit.
Arogya Sanjeevani Co-pay: 5%.
Day Care: Requires less than 24 hours.

cards.title: Q1. Reimburses actual bills?
cards.content: Answer: Indemnity Policy.

cards.title: Q2. Family Floater Premium calculation?
cards.content: Answer: Based on oldest member's age.

cards.title: Risk Assessment Focus
cards.content:
• Concept of Morbidity vs. Mortality
• Medical, Non-Medical & Tele-underwriting
• Fraud Management (Hard vs. Soft Fraud)
• Anti-selection Prevention

cards.title: Key Definition
cards.content:
Mortality: Probability of Death.
Morbidity: Probability of falling ill/injured. 
Health insurance focuses 100% on MORBIDITY risk.

cards.title: 1. Tele-underwriting
cards.content: Gathering medical history over the phone by doctors. Speeds up policy issuance and reduces physical tests.

cards.title: 2. Medical vs Non-Medical
cards.content:
Medical: Requires physical tests (ECG, Blood test).
Non-Medical: Issued based only on proposal form declarations.

cards.title: Anti-Selection
cards.content: The tendency of sick people to buy insurance. Underwriting prevents this to protect the company.

cards.title: Hard vs Soft Fraud
cards.content:
Hard Fraud: Deliberate (Fake bills for treatment that NEVER happened).
Soft Fraud: Exaggerated (Staying in hospital longer than needed just to claim more).

sections.title: 🚀 Exam Pro-Tips (Repeated Themes)
items: Health Underwriting Focus: Morbidity.
Sick people buying insurance: Anti-selection.
Phone call medical history: Tele-underwriting.
Fake documents: Hard Fraud.

cards.title: Q1. Health underwriting primary focus?
cards.content: Answer: Morbidity.

cards.title: Q2. Fake bill for no treatment?
cards.content: Answer: Hard Fraud.

cards.title: Practical Processes
cards.content:
• Role of TPA (Third Party Administrator)
• Cashless vs. Reimbursement Claims
• Pre (30 Days) & Post (60 Days) Hospitalization
• Network vs. Non-Network Hospitals

cards.title: 1. Cashless vs Reimbursement
cards.content:
Cashless: At Network Hospitals only. Insurer pays hospital directly.
Reimbursement: At Non-Network Hospitals. Patient pays first, then gets refund.

cards.title: 2. Role of TPA
cards.content: Licensed by IRDAI to process claims and issue ID cards. CRITICAL: Risk/Liability always rests with the Insurance Co, NOT the TPA.

cards.title: Pre & Post Expenses
cards.content:
Pre-Hospitalization: 30 DAYS before admission.
Post-Hospitalization: 60 DAYS after discharge.

cards.title: Claim Intimation
cards.content:
Emergency: Inform within 24 hours.
Planned: Inform 48-72 hours before admission.

sections.title: 🚀 Exam Pro-Tips (Repeated Themes)
items: Pre-Hospitalization period: 30 Days.
Post-Hospitalization period: 60 Days.
ID Card Issuer: TPA.
TPA Risk: None (Financial risk is with Insurer).

cards.title: Q1. Non-network hospital claim type?
cards.content: Answer: Reimbursement Claim.

cards.title: Q2. Standard Pre-Hospitalization limit?
cards.content: Answer: 30 Days.
